name: Build a Profile Card Generator
scenario: You're building a feature for a developer community platform (think dev.to, GitHub, Hashnode). Each user has a 'profile card' that shows their stats and basic info. Your job: write a Java program that generates a formatted profile card for any developer. The output should look like it could be a real feature on a real platform.
description: Build a program that stores a developer's profile information using the correct data types, calculates a few derived values, and prints a clean, professional-looking profile card. This is your chance to apply everything from Topic 2: choosing types, using constants, calculating values, and formatting output.

core.description: Compiles and runs without errors or warnings
Uses at least 6 different variables with correctly chosen types (must include String, int, double, boolean, and at least one of: char, long)
Has at least 2 calculated values (e.g., average rating, total contributions, days since joined)
Output has clear visual structure (borders, sections, alignment)
Variable names are descriptive and follow camelCase convention

bonus.description: Includes 'engagement score' calculation using multiple variables (e.g., (posts*5 + comments*2 + likes) / accountAgeInDays)
Uses long type appropriately (e.g., total views in millions, account ID)
Includes a badge/status calculation based on stats (e.g., 'PRO', 'VERIFIED', based on follower count)
Uses ASCII art or Unicode characters creatively for visual flair

data_to_use:
Use this developer profile (or invent your own — but use realistic numbers):

- Name: Your choice (use a realistic name)
- Username: Developer's handle (e.g., 'priya_codes')
- Title: e.g., 'Backend Developer'
- Years of experience: 3
- Number of public repos: 47
- Total followers: 1284
- Total posts: 89
- Average post rating: 4.7 (out of 5.0)
- Is verified developer: true
- Account ID: a long number
- Tier badge: 'P' for Pro (single char)
- Daily posts limit (constant): 10
- Max followers free tier (constant): 5000
example_output:
╔═══════════════════════════════════════╗
║         DEVELOPER PROFILE             ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════╝
  Name:        Priya Sharma
  Username:    @priya_codes
  Title:       Backend Developer
  Tier:        [P] Pro Member
  Verified:    true
  Account ID:  894523741892
---------------------------------------
  Experience:  3 years
  Repos:       47
  Posts:       89
  Followers:   1284
  Rating:      4.7 / 5.0
---------------------------------------
  Engagement Score: 12.5
  Daily Limit:      10 posts
  Follower Cap:     5000 (free tier)
=======================================

criteria: int used for whole numbers in range (5 pts)
long or char used appropriately (5 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: AI hints are allowed but limited to 5 for this project. Try to think through type choices yourself first — this is the topic's whole point. Use hints when truly stuck. Don't ask AI to generate the full solution.
submission_instructions: Save your code in ProfileCard.java. Run locally or in our editor. Verify your output is clean and professional. Submit through the platform for auto-grading. Add this to your GitHub portfolio repo — this is real-looking work you can show off.
tips_for_success: Plan first. Before coding, list every variable you need and decide its type. This is a senior-engineer habit.
Use realistic numbers. A developer with 5 billion followers isn't believable.
Don't make the engagement score formula too complex — pick 2-3 inputs that make logical sense.
Test your output by running it. Does it look like something a real platform would display?
Add comments above each section explaining what it does. Future you (and reviewers) will appreciate it.
If you push to GitHub, write a good commit message: 'feat: developer profile card generator with engagement scoring'
concepts_reinforced: Using all primitive types in context
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: Building this teaches you something subtle but critical: TYPE CHOICE IS A DESIGN DECISION. Every variable you declare is a small architectural choice. Should follower count be int or long? Could it ever exceed 2 billion? Should rating be float or double? Will the precision matter? These tiny decisions, multiplied across thousands of variables in a real project, define whether your software is robust or fragile. Top engineers think about types deliberately. Junior engineers default to int/double for everything and ship bugs.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Read profile data from a config file (we'll cover file I/O later — flag for now)
Multiple profile cards in one run (preview of arrays/loops, coming in Topic 4)
Add input validation (e.g., negative followers shouldn't be allowed)
Export profile as JSON-like string format
Calculate profile completeness score (% of fields filled)
